zh:
印度神话是世界上仍在被亿万人日常信奉的最古老神话体系之一。它的源头是约公元前1500年起口传的四部吠陀——《梨俱吠陀》一千余首颂歌里，雷霆之王因陀罗斩杀旱魃巨蛇、火神阿耆尼传递祭品、苏摩酒被称作不死甘露。此后近三千年，这套神话经由两大史诗《摩诃婆罗多》（约十万颂，内含《薄伽梵歌》）与《罗摩衍那》（约两万四千颂），再到中世纪的十八部往世书，不断层累、改写与扩张：吠陀时代的主神因陀罗渐渐让位，梵天、毗湿奴、湿婆组成的三相神分掌创造、维护与毁灭，女神传统又在《女神颂》中汇成杜尔迦与迦梨的战斗身影。

这套体系的底色是循环：宇宙在梵天的一日之间生灭，时间以四个由盛而衰的时代（yuga）滚动，每一轮毁灭之后紧接着新一轮创造。提婆（天神）与阿修罗（魔神）是同父异母的兄弟，为不死甘露既合作搅动乳海又反目相夺；毗湿奴的十大化身依次降世，鱼、龟、野猪、人狮，直到罗摩与克里希那这样有血有肉的英雄。神明有配偶、坐骑与家庭，湿婆的儿子是象头的迦尼萨，毗湿奴的坐骑金翅鸟与蛇族世代为敌——神谱同时是一张亲缘与恩怨之网。

它也是流传范围最广的神话体系之一：随佛教东传，因陀罗成为汉传佛典中的帝释天，须弥山撑起整个东亚佛教宇宙观，那伽（龙）与迦楼罗位列天龙八部，罗刹成为汉语里食人恶鬼的通称；向南，《罗摩衍那》衍生出泰国《拉玛坚》与柬埔寨《里姆克》，吴哥窟的长廊浮雕至今搅动着乳海。而在印度本土，这些故事从不是遗迹——每年秋天，加尔各答为杜尔迦搭起数千祭棚，孟买把十万尊象头神像送入海中，大壶节则以上亿人次的沐浴延续着对那一壶甘露的记忆。
en:
Hindu mythology is among the oldest bodies of myth still worshipped daily by hundreds of millions. Its wellspring is the four Vedas, transmitted orally from around 1500 BCE: in the thousand-odd hymns of the Rigveda, Indra the thunderer slays the drought serpent, Agni carries offerings to heaven, and the soma draught is already called amrita, the deathless. Over the next three millennia the tradition grew by accretion—through the two great epics, the Mahabharata (some 100,000 verses, containing the Bhagavad Gita) and the Ramayana (some 24,000), and on into the eighteen medieval Puranas. The Vedic king Indra gradually yielded pride of place to the Trimurti—Brahma the creator, Vishnu the preserver, Shiva the destroyer—while the goddess tradition crystallized in the Devi Mahatmya into the battle forms of Durga and Kali.

The deep pattern of this cosmos is the cycle. Universes are born and dissolved within a single day of Brahma; time rolls through four ages of steepening decline; each dissolution opens straight into the next creation. The devas and asuras, gods and anti-gods, are half-brothers who first cooperate to churn the ocean of milk for the nectar of immortality and then fight over the prize. Vishnu descends in ten successive avatars—fish, tortoise, boar, man-lion—down to flesh-and-blood heroes like Rama and Krishna. The gods have spouses, mounts and quarrelsome families: Shiva's son is the elephant-headed Ganesha, and Vishnu's eagle Garuda wages an eternal feud with the serpent nagas, so that the pantheon reads as a web of kinship and grudge.

Few mythologies have travelled farther. Carried east with Buddhism, Indra became Sakra of the Chinese scriptures, Mount Meru came to anchor the entire East Asian Buddhist cosmos, nagas and Garuda joined the eight legions of dharma protectors, and the rakshasa entered Chinese as the luocha, a byword for man-eating fiends. Southward, the Ramayana became Thailand's Ramakien and Cambodia's Reamker, and the ocean of milk is still being churned along the galleries of Angkor Wat. In India itself these stories were never ruins: each autumn Kolkata raises thousands of pavilions for Durga, Mumbai carries a hundred thousand Ganesha images into the sea, and the Kumbh Mela draws bathers by the hundred million in memory of a single pot of nectar.
